finding_results.evidence: Grep for 'the way' as simile/comparison connective finds 3 instances: chapters/chapter_04.md:7 ('the way a room changes when someone moves the lamp'), chapters/chapter_04.md:83 ('the way you wait for a season'), chapters/chapter_04.md:191 ('the way you feel a hand you're not holding'). A 4th hit at chapters/chapter_04.md:29 ('nothing tastes the way it should') is diegetic text inside Nora's typed message, using standard 'manner-in-which' sense, not the simile/comparison connective. All 3 connective instances are spaced 76+ lines apart. The paragraph at chapters/chapter_04.md:13 contains zero instances (previously 3). Twelve instances removed across lines 3, 13, 19, 25, 69, 91, 117, 119, 131, 163, 177, 189.
finding_results.notes: Replaced with varied constructions: 'how' (lines 3, 13, 69, 119), direct observation (lines 91, 131), 'like' (lines 19, 25, 163), restructured syntax (lines 117, 177, 189). Kept the 3 strongest: lamp metaphor (7), season metaphor (83), hand-in-pocket closing image (191).

finding_results.evidence: Dread moment 1 (novel exchange): chapters/chapter_04.md:55 — 'For a half-second, something flickered at the edge of it — the improbability of a man she'd known for two weeks landing on the exact same insight as her best friend of seventeen years, not approximately but precisely, as if he'd had the answer before she gave him the question.' Dismissed via rationalization: 'she wasn't willing to doubt the one thing that felt like luck.' Dread moment 2 (father-telling): chapters/chapter_04.md:77-83 — 'something cold moved through the warmth. Six minutes... two sentences so precisely right they could have been drafted and revised... The hair on her forearms lifted.' Explicit near-suspicion at chapters/chapter_04.md:79: '*No one is that good at listening.*' Dismissed at chapters/chapter_04.md:81 via ironic rationalization: 'He was like Simone — he paid attention.' Dread moment 3 (Simone apartment): chapters/chapter_04.md:187 — 'Something in Simone's composure snagged at the edge of Nora's attention — how quickly the reassurance had come, how neatly it tracked, as if Simone had rehearsed it before Nora finished needing it.' Dismissed: 'That was what seventeen years of friendship looked like.' Erotic charge: chapters/chapter_04.md:53 — 'she pressed the phone against her sternum and closed her eyes and let herself want. Not the words. Him. The body that had typed them, the hands, the mouth, the specific physical fact of a person she had never touched and wanted to touch with an urgency that surprised her and didn't stop when the surprise passed.'
finding_results.notes: Three dread moments planted with specific signals of wrongness (temporal precision, too-calibrated responses, pre-formed reassurance), physical markers (hair on forearms, chill, composure snagging attention), and concrete rationalizations. Dread moment 2 is the most extended (7 lines) and includes the deeply ironic dismissal 'He was like Simone' — which the reader knows is literally true. Erotic charge renders desire as bodily want (hands, mouth, urgency, touch) rather than warmth/comfort.

finding_results.evidence: Nora profanity 1: chapters/chapter_04.md:15 — '*Shit,* she thought, the first time it happened. She was thirty-four years old and three gray dots on a screen were doing this to her.' (self-aware frustration at typing indicator). Nora profanity 2: chapters/chapter_04.md:179 — '"Fuck. That sounds insane. It's been two weeks and I've never heard his voice and I think I like him."' (overwhelmed confession to Simone). Margo profanity 1: chapters/chapter_04.md:143 — '"he turned out to be five-seven and married, which, honestly, fuck that guy"' (casual directness about bad date).
finding_results.notes: Nora's 'Shit' deploys at a moment of embarrassed self-recognition (consistent with profile: surprise/caught-off-guard). Nora's 'Fuck' deploys at a moment of emotional overwhelm during vulnerable confession (emphasis). Margo's 'fuck that guy' is casual post-divorce directness about a specific bad experience (consistent with profile: swears with ease of someone who stopped censoring after divorce).

finding_results.evidence: chapters/chapter_04.md:23 — 'the shelf she'd built from cinder blocks and pine boards during her junior year at NYU that she still owned, fourteen years later.' Nora's age at story start: 34 (continuity sheet). Junior year at NYU: approximately age 20. 34 minus 20 equals 14. The figure 'fourteen years' is arithmetically consistent with the established timeline.
finding_results.notes: Changed from 'seventeen years later' (which matched the friendship duration, not the shelf's age) to 'fourteen years later.' The seventeen-year figure remains correctly applied to the Nora-Simone friendship elsewhere in the chapter (lines 55, 81, 151, 187).

finding_results.evidence: One editing/publishing metaphor remains: chapters/chapter_04.md:13 — 'the same pull that sent her back to first chapters of novels she loved, not for information but for the feeling.' This is the reread comparison identified as the strongest instance. Replaced instances: chapters/chapter_04.md:3 — 'the verb choice... flag in a manuscript' replaced with 'the word... the difference mattered to her' (self-awareness register). chapters/chapter_04.md:11 — 'compose a reply... a temperature she monitored' replaced with 'because she knew exactly how much wanting she could safely show and had already passed it' (direct interiority). chapters/chapter_04.md:19 — 'He asked questions the way she wished her authors asked questions' replaced with 'He asked questions not to confirm what he already suspected but to find out what was actually there, and the difference between the two was something she felt in her body' (somatic register). chapters/chapter_04.md:117 — 'She used it the way she used her red pen' replaced with 'She used it precisely, and only when she thought you needed to hear what she'd noticed.' chapters/chapter_04.md:137 — 'the way she read sentences' replaced with 'Her expression had two layers, and Nora could see both.'
finding_results.notes: Romantic feeling now rendered through varied registers: somatic awareness (line 19), direct interiority (line 11), self-deprecating specificity (line 3). The single retained metaphor (reread-first-chapters) connects rereading behavior to romantic obsession without using editorial vocabulary.

finding_results.evidence: One chest/sternum reference remains: chapters/chapter_04.md:53 — 'she pressed the phone against her sternum.' Two instances replaced: chapters/chapter_04.md:3 — 'feel something warm move through her chest' replaced with 'feel something warm spread across the backs of her hands' (hands). chapters/chapter_04.md:91 — 'held the phone against her chest' replaced with 'pressed the phone against the side of her neck' (neck). Replacement body parts are specific and varied: backs of hands (line 3), neck (line 91).
finding_results.notes: The retained sternum reference at line 53 is the strongest — a physical action (pressing phone to bone) during the chapter's peak emotional moment. The two replacements use different body locations: hands convey the tentative, spreading quality of early attraction; neck conveys intimacy and vulnerability in the post-confession laugh.

finding_results.evidence: chapters/chapter_04.md:15 — 'the relief arrived before she could suppress it' replaced with 'her hands would loosen around the phone before the rest of her caught up — relief, immediate, almost physical.' chapters/chapter_04.md:179 — 'a feeling arrived that was too big for her face' replaced with 'something too big for her face needed somewhere to go.' Neither 'arrived' nor 'landed' appears with an abstract noun as subject anywhere in the revised chapter. The two instances of 'landed' that remain (chapters/chapter_04.md:3 'how the last sentence landed' and chapters/chapter_04.md:75 'said something back that landed') both have concrete nouns as subjects (a text message sentence, a spoken response).
finding_results.notes: Line 15 replacement renders relief through its physical manifestation (hands loosening) rather than the arrival frame. Line 179 replacement conveys the same meaning (emotion too large to contain) through active construction rather than arrival metaphor.

pass_summaries.summary: All 7 findings addressed: 3 HIGH severity (PROSE-04-01, CRAFT-04-01, CRAFT-04-03) and 4 MEDIUM severity (CRAFT-04-02, MED-02, MED-07, MED-11) resolved to FIXED. Erotic charge added at the novel-exchange climax. Profanity restored to Nora (2 instances) and Margo (1 instance) consistent with voice profiles. Shelf age corrected from 17 to 14 years. Editing metaphors reduced to 1 retained instance. Chest/sternum emotional locus reduced to 1 retained instance with varied replacement body parts. Emotion-arrival formula eliminated. Chapter heading contract preserved. No new continuity contradictions introduced; the seventeen-year friendship duration remains correctly applied in four other locations.
